techniques.title: Play the Victim Card
techniques.shortDefinition: Tell a personal hardship story to win sympathy and make the judge feel protective toward your side.
techniques.howToSpot: Heavy focus on feelings and suffering, with vivid details, while evidence about the motion is thin or missing.
techniques.howToRebut: Acknowledge feelings briefly, then bring the debate back to fair standards: burdens, evidence, and what policy helps most students—not one story alone.
techniques.caution: If overused, it can sound manipulative; in rebuttal, stay respectful.

techniques.title: Appeal to the Underdog
techniques.shortDefinition: Highlight a sympathetic small player versus a powerful villain so the audience wants to side with you emotionally.
techniques.howToSpot: Big contrast (e.g., giant company vs one local shop) designed to trigger fairness feelings instead of comparing realistic options.
techniques.howToRebut: Show nuance: you can support small actors without accepting their whole policy. Ask whether the rule is fair on balance and what unintended harm their plan creates.

techniques.title: Strategic Concession
techniques.shortDefinition: Admit a small weakness of your side, then pivot to a stronger argument that still wins the clash.
techniques.howToSpot: “We agree X, BUT…” then a tight analogy or principle that reframes the debate in their favor.
techniques.howToRebut: Do not let the concession swallow the whole case. Show the “but” is a non sequitur, the analogy breaks, or their “principle” still loses on impacts.

techniques.title: Frame the Debate
techniques.shortDefinition: Redefine what the round is “really about” to shrink the question to one issue they think they win.
techniques.howToSpot: “This debate is NOT about… The ONLY question is…” often without proving that narrow frame is fair.
techniques.howToRebut: Defend a fair frame: list stakeholders and clashes the motion must cover. Explain why their frame is cherry-picking or avoids the opposition’s core harm.

techniques.title: Create False Dichotomy
techniques.shortDefinition: Present only two extreme choices and pressure the judge to pick theirs.
techniques.howToSpot: “We have two choices…” with no middle path, regulation, education, or phased reform.
techniques.howToRebut: Offer a third way (or spectrum). Show both extremes are straw versions of the debate and your model captures real-world trade-offs.

techniques.title: Good Cop, Bad Cop
techniques.shortDefinition: In 2v2, one speaker is harsh and emotional while the teammate sounds reasonable—steering judges toward the “moderate” teammate’s position.
techniques.howToSpot: Sharp tone swing between partners; the “reasonable” voice still smuggles the same conclusion.
techniques.howToRebut: Name the tactic politely: both speakers share one burden. Attack the logical link, not just the angry delivery, and show the “moderate” case still fails on evidence.

techniques.title: Tag-Team Rebuttal
techniques.shortDefinition: Partners split rebuttal lanes (e.g., numbers vs fairness) so each clash gets a specialist response.
techniques.howToSpot: Clear division of labor; minimal overlap; may feel coordinated in a polished way.
techniques.howToRebut: If you are solo practice: learn to map clashes. In round: exploit gaps—if Speaker 2 answered fairness weakly, press that clash and note inconsistency across speeches.

techniques.title: Build on Each Other
techniques.shortDefinition: Speaker 2 explicitly extends Speaker 1’s point with “My teammate mentioned… let me add…” to look unified.
techniques.howToSpot: Repeated bridging phrases adding new benefits or mechanisms on top of the first speech.
techniques.howToRebut: Check whether the “add” is new evidence or just new adjectives. If it is cumulative rhetoric without proof, call that out and collapse the stack to their weakest link.

techniques.title: Sacrifice Pawn, Protect King
techniques.shortDefinition: Let one weaker claim absorb attacks while the team redirects to their strongest core claim.
techniques.howToSpot: “Even if you disagree with X, our main point is Y…” after X is challenged.
techniques.howToRebut: Show Y still depends on X, or that Y is unsupported. Do not chase the pawn forever—explain why losing X collapses Y.

techniques.title: Cross-Examination Trap
techniques.shortDefinition: Ask a question that forces a simple “yes,” then use that answer against them in a later speech.
techniques.howToSpot: Overgeneral yes/no setup; later: “Even they agreed…”
techniques.howToRebut: Qualify carefully in CX: define terms, add scope. In rebuttal: explain the yes was narrow and their conclusion does not follow.

techniques.title: Red Herring
techniques.shortDefinition: Introduce an emotional or vivid side topic to dodge the actual clash.
techniques.howToSpot: A touching story or moral outrage that does not answer the opponent’s mechanism or evidence.
techniques.howToRebut: Label the shift: “That is a different issue.” Return to the unanswered argument and restate why it matters under the motion.

techniques.title: Straw Man Attack
techniques.shortDefinition: Exaggerate or distort the other side’s position to make it easier to knock down.
techniques.howToSpot: “They want to ban everything / live in caves…” when they proposed limits, not bans.
techniques.howToRebut: Quote their actual line or model. Show the gap between their claim and your caricature, then answer the real argument.

techniques.title: Slippery Slope Amplification
techniques.shortDefinition: Chain small steps to a scary extreme without showing each step is likely.
techniques.howToSpot: “Today X, tomorrow Y, next Z…” with no causal evidence or safeguards.
techniques.howToRebut: Demand warrants: why each step follows, where policy can stop, and what real-world examples show moderation works.

techniques.title: Tu Quoque (“You Too”)
techniques.shortDefinition: Attack the speaker’s personal inconsistency instead of answering the argument.
techniques.howToSpot: “You did it too…” irrelevant to whether the idea is good for society.
techniques.howToRebut: Separate character from claim: even if imperfect, the argument can still be tested on logic and evidence. Redirect to standards.

techniques.title: Burden of Proof Shift
techniques.shortDefinition: Demand the other side “prove” an impossible guarantee, then claim victory when they cannot.
techniques.howToSpot: “Can they PROVE/GUARANTEE…?” on complex futures; shifting standards unfairly.
techniques.howToRebut: State the fair burden on each side. Use preponderance of evidence, comparative worlds, or reasonable safety margins—not magic certainty.

techniques.title: Anchor the Debate
techniques.shortDefinition: Pick a memorable number or slogan and repeat it so all other numbers look “extreme.”
techniques.howToSpot: A bold figure repeated; opponents’ alternatives framed as far from the anchor.
techniques.howToRebut: Challenge why that anchor is justified. Offer a better metric or show their number is arbitrary or harmful on hidden margins.

techniques.title: Silence as a Weapon
techniques.shortDefinition: Use deliberate pauses after key lines to pressure the room and magnify doubt.
techniques.howToSpot: Rhythmic speech with long pauses around punchy claims; emotion over explanation.
techniques.howToRebut: In writing/feedback practice: ignore theatrics; ask “what was the warrant?” In live rounds: fill silence with a calm, precise answer to the claim they implied.

techniques.title: Name the Judge
techniques.shortDefinition: Direct moral pressure at the adjudicator: “Judge, you must choose…”
techniques.howToSpot: Repeated second-person appeals to the chair with dramatic contrasts.
techniques.howToRebut: Stay professional: remind what decision criteria are (analysis, weighing), and why your side wins on those—not guilt.

techniques.title: Emotional Labeling
techniques.shortDefinition: Stick negative labels on the other team’s motive (selfish, lazy, cruel) without proof.
techniques.howToSpot: Adjectives about character or intent instead of clash engagement.
techniques.howToRebut: Reject labels; restate your principle in neutral terms; show their label misreads your actual case.

techniques.title: The Power of Pause
techniques.shortDefinition: Pause before answering an opponent to suggest their point is weak or absurd.
techniques.howToSpot: Body language + silence used to dramatize; may replace a substantive answer.
techniques.howToRebut: Do not be rattled—answer clearly. If they paused instead of engaging, point out missing responses.

techniques.title: Drop the Mic Moment
techniques.shortDefinition: A highly memorable, parallel final paragraph designed to stick in the judge’s mind.
techniques.howToSpot: Rhythmic repetition, antithesis, slogan-like endings; style over new analysis.
techniques.howToRebut: Deconstruct slogans: show parallel structure hid missing links or false equivalence between ideas.

techniques.title: Pre-empt the Judge’s Doubt
techniques.shortDefinition: Name the judge’s likely worry, then answer it with a quick example or statistic.
techniques.howToSpot: “You might think… but…” often in closing speeches.
techniques.howToRebut: Show their example is cherry-picked, outdated, or not comparable to your context; raise a deeper doubt they did not address.

techniques.title: Compare Worlds
techniques.shortDefinition: Paint two futures (“In their world… in our world…”) to force a binary choice.
techniques.howToSpot: Vivid extremes; little middle ground; emotional imagery.
techniques.howToRebut: Challenge the futures as unrealistic straw worlds. Offer a comparative analysis with trade-offs and probabilities.

techniques.title: The Rule of Three Finale
techniques.shortDefinition: Three parallel lines (problem / solution / call) for rhythm and memorability.
techniques.howToSpot: Triadic structure in closing; may recycle known points without new weighing.
techniques.howToRebut: Weigh: which of the three claims is weakest? If one falls, show why the rhythm does not save the case.

techniques.title: Call Back to Opening
techniques.shortDefinition: Return to a story or example from first speech to create narrative closure.
techniques.howToSpot: “Remember Lily…?” tying vote to that character.
techniques.howToRebut: Show the story is not representative, or outcomes do not follow from the policy; separate anecdote from general principle.

techniques.title: Turn Their Evidence Against Them
techniques.shortDefinition: Reinterpret their statistic so it supports your side.
techniques.howToSpot: Clever reframing that may ignore nuance in the study.
techniques.howToRebut: Cite methodology, scope, and what the study actually recommends; explain why their reinterpretation misreads the source.

techniques.title: Expose the Hidden Assumption
techniques.shortDefinition: Make an unstated premise explicit, then attack that premise.
techniques.howToSpot: “They assume X=Y…” then counterexamples.
techniques.howToRebut: Defend the assumption with warrants, or show your case does not rely on the brittle link they attacked.

techniques.title: The “Even If” Defense
techniques.shortDefinition: Grant a point for argument’s sake, then show you still win a bigger clash.
techniques.howToSpot: “EVEN IF… still…” structure.
techniques.howToRebut: Show the grant is not enough: their “bigger clash” is smaller than they say, or depends on facts they did not grant.

techniques.title: Point Out the Real Stakes
techniques.shortDefinition: Reframe the round as a moral choice between two values (e.g., health vs scores).
techniques.howToSpot: “This is not about X, it is about…” with loaded contrasts.
techniques.howToRebut: Reject false value choices: show compatible goals, measurement, or a policy that serves both better than their dichotomy.

techniques.title: The Final Question
techniques.shortDefinition: End with a rhetorical question they “cannot answer,” then assert there is no answer.
techniques.howToSpot: One zinger question + “They can’t answer…” without fair time to respond.
techniques.howToRebut: Answer the question directly and simply, or show it compares incomparable cases / uses a flawed analogy.

techniques.title: Hasty Generalization
techniques.shortDefinition: Draw a broad rule from too few examples or an unrepresentative sample.
techniques.howToSpot: “One time… therefore always…” or “I know a person who…” as the whole proof.
techniques.howToRebut: Ask for representativeness and scale: larger samples, mechanisms, and what typical cases look like.

techniques.title: False Cause (Faulty Attribution)
techniques.shortDefinition: Assume A caused B because B came after A, or confuse correlation with causation.
techniques.howToSpot: “After the policy, scores rose—so the policy worked,” with no control for other factors.
techniques.howToRebut: Demand a causal story: confounders, incentives, and alternative explanations; propose what evidence would separate causes.

techniques.title: False Analogy
techniques.shortDefinition: Compare two situations that differ in the features that matter for the conclusion.
techniques.howToSpot: Catchy metaphor that breaks on key differences (scale, risk, rights, reversibility).
techniques.howToRebut: Map similarities and differences; explain which differences defeat the analogy and what better comparison fits.

techniques.title: Appeal to Authority (Fallacious)
techniques.shortDefinition: Cite an expert or title outside their real area, or treat a slogan as proof.
techniques.howToSpot: Name-dropping without study details, or authority on topic A used to settle unrelated topic B.
techniques.howToRebut: Check domain match and whether experts disagree; ask for the actual reasoning or data behind the claim.

techniques.title: Bandwagon Appeal
techniques.shortDefinition: Argue something is true or good because “everyone” believes or does it.
techniques.howToSpot: Peer pressure, trends, popularity—without independent reasons.
techniques.howToRebut: Popularity is not proof: give counterexamples, minorities’ rights, or cases where crowds were wrong.

techniques.title: Begging the Question (Circular Reasoning)
techniques.shortDefinition: Assume the conclusion inside the premise, just with different words.
techniques.howToSpot: “It is bad because it is wrong,” or definitions that already smuggle the outcome.
techniques.howToRebut: Restate the argument in simple form and show the premise and conclusion are the same claim needing independent support.
